does: This paper introduces Prompt Embedding Optimization (PEO), a white-box jailbreak method that directly optimizes the continuous embeddings of original prompt tokens to elicit harmful outputs from aligned language models. Unlike prior discrete suffix-appending attacks, PEO modifies embeddings in the continuous space while preserving the visible prompt text through nearest-token projection after each optimization round. The method employs multi-round optimization with structured continuation targets and an adaptive failure-focused schedule that concentrates computational effort on previously failed attack attempts. The authors demonstrate that embedding perturbations can remain sufficiently small to maintain semantic coherence and topical relevance while successfully bypassing safety alignments, challenging the assumption that embedding-space attacks inherently destroy prompt meaning.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research exploring adversarial attacks against aligned language models, extending beyond discrete token-level suffix attacks like ARCA and AutoPrompt that operate in combinatorial spaces with limited flexibility. It responds to the implicit assumption in prior work that directly perturbing embeddings of semantic tokens would catastrophically corrupt prompts, instead drawing on insights from continuous adversarial optimization in computer vision and the embedding-space robustness literature. The paper connects to foundational work on finding adversarial examples in neural networks while adapting these techniques to the specific challenge of jailbreaking aligned LLMs without leaving visible evidence of tampering, and relates to recent research examining the gap between discrete and continuous perturbation attacks.
enables: This research opens pathways for understanding the robustness of alignment mechanisms against subtle, imperceptible modifications to model inputs, suggesting that safety training may leave exploitable vulnerabilities in the continuous representation space. It enables future work on developing defenses specifically targeting embedding-space perturbations, potentially requiring new alignment techniques that robustify continuous representations rather than just optimizing behavior at the token level. The work also points toward investigating whether similar embedding-space attacks could transfer across models, and raises questions about detecting or auditing whether model inputs have been adversarially modified in their continuous spaces without leaving traces in the visible text.
